title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
text:
This series examines why early-stage technical founders with strong ideas and credible science often struggle to advance their pitches. Each piece isolates a different repeated pattern of mistakes across university spinouts and science-driven startups.
By the end, we’ll have articulated an investor’s mindset when they evaluate a technical pitch- making the entire thought-process legible so founders can know the rules of the inscrutable game investors are playing.
At Georgetown Tech Ventures, I work with early-stage academic startups and university spinouts every day—that means watching, reviewing, and refining pitches from faculty, graduate students, first-time founders, and newly formed teams. I watch them try, win or lose, to transmute research into companies.
It also means sitting on evaluation panels, judging pitch competitions, and seeing how different audiences can react differently to the same pitch.. It’s my job to sit, listen, question, and observe- the founders, the context, the science, and the outcomes. After a while, the patterns of behaviors that separate wins from losses have become undeniable:
Smart people. Serious work. Strong science. And yet, the pitch misses—not because the idea is bad, but because the pitch answers a different question than the one the audience is asking.
Earlier in my career, I was on the other side of the table; I ran a company and pitched to investors. Those accumulated experiences crystallized an idea: what compels founders often does not compel investors to commit time, resources, credibility, or capital.
This series is written from the accumulation of those experiences then and now. This isn’t pitch coaching, presentation advice, or a checklist for founders to “become better storytellers.”
This series explains how early-stage pitches are actually evaluated—and why so many well-intentioned founders, especially technical ones, systematically miss the decision they’re trying to influence.

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
text:
This series examines why early-stage technical founders with strong ideas and credible science often struggle to advance their pitches. Each piece isolates a different repeated pattern of mistakes across university spinouts and science-driven startups.
By the end, we’ll have articulated an investor’s mindset when they evaluate a technical pitch- making the entire thought-process legible so founders can know the rules of the inscrutable game investors are playing.
At Georgetown Tech Ventures, I work with early-stage academic startups and university spinouts every day—that means watching, reviewing, and refining pitches from faculty, graduate students, first-time founders, and newly formed teams. I watch them try, win or lose, to transmute research into companies.
It also means sitting on evaluation panels, judging pitch competitions, and seeing how different audiences can react differently to the same pitch.. It’s my job to sit, listen, question, and observe- the founders, the context, the science, and the outcomes. After a while, the patterns of behaviors that separate wins from losses have become undeniable:
Smart people. Serious work. Strong science. And yet, the pitch misses—not because the idea is bad, but because the pitch answers a different question than the one the audience is asking.
Earlier in my career, I was on the other side of the table; I ran a company and pitched to investors. Those accumulated experiences crystallized an idea: what compels founders often does not compel investors to commit time, resources, credibility, or capital.
This series is written from the accumulation of those experiences then and now. This isn’t pitch coaching, presentation advice, or a checklist for founders to “become better storytellers.”
This series explains how early-stage pitches are actually evaluated—and why so many well-intentioned founders, especially technical ones, systematically miss the decision they’re trying to influence.

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
text:
What Founders Think Pitching Is
Most early-stage technical founders—particularly those coming out of academic environments—approach pitching like a poster presentation.
The instinct is straightforward: lay out the problem, describe the solution, and walk through the underlying research—often in unintentionally excruciating detail.
The presumption being that with enough context, the right conclusion should follow and the value will be obvious.
The instinct isn’t careless. It’s an engrained belief trained over decades and perpetuated by mentors, peers, and expectant subordinates: .
Credibility is earned by demonstrating rigor and command of detail. Posters, papers, grant applications, dissertation defenses—these are formats where explaining the science is the work.
Precision is rewarded. Completeness signals seriousness. The ability to defend assumptions is the measure of competence.
So, when these founders pitch, they do what humans do best: fall back on the trained behaviors they know work.
If pitching was a question of science we wouldn’t need investors- we’d have more grant panels.
Founders often assume the audience is engaging like a reviewer or examiner—that the primary task in the room is comprehension.
But pitch audiences, and the investors that compose them, are not trying to understand because investors are not in the business of understanding. Investors are in the business of deciding:
Whether to keep listening or engage further. Whether to allocate scarce time, attention, credibility, or capital to the opportunity presented to them right now.
As a lifelong cinephile, allow me to put this in familiar terms,

A pitch is more akin to a movie trailer: it’s selective and compressed; a few scenes, a sense of stakes, and a reason to keep watching.
What I see technical founders actually make is a director’s commentary: a long-form explanation to an already invested audience that wants to understand how the movie came to be.

Most pitch rooms are made for movie trailers, even if founders show up with their commentary track.
Until that misalignment is addressed, no amount of refinement helps:

You can explain better and still stall.
You can add slides and still lose momentum.
You can articulate extraordinary science and still fail to trigger a decision.

To understand why, we first have to be clear.

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
section: A pitch is NOT a poster presentation.
text:
A pitch is NOT a poster presentation.
A poster is designed for depth. It rewards completeness. It invites detours.
For example, if someone wants to zoom in on a methodological choice, a control condition, or an edge case, that’s a feature—not a flaw.
The discussion can meander in the manner as the path of discovery; never linear, never expected, and it’s a richer discussion for it.
A pitch isn’t built for that.

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
section: A pitch is NOT a white paper.
text:
A pitch is NOT a white paper.
White papers assume time.
They assume rereading. They assume the reader can pause, backtrack, annotate, and come back later. They are meant to be referenced and absorbed. They can carry nuance because the format allows for it.
A pitch doesn’t allow for that kind of attention.

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
section: And a pitch is NOT a grant application.
text:
And a pitch is NOT a grant application.
Grant logic is about justification.
It’s a case for continued research that is often evaluated by people who are trained to reward technical merit and methodological thoroughness.
Pitch logic is similar, but the audience is very different. Investors are not deciding whether your work is worthy of continued research. They’re deciding whether it’s worth it to them; their time, their attention, their credibility, their capital—right now, and relative to everything else— they could support instead.
The science never fails in these scenarios. The pitch does.
Founders bring the instincts that earned them success in one arena into a room governed by an entirely different set of rules.
It’s like dropping a pole vaulter into a hockey game—they could be world-class, but they didn’t train to take a hit from a six-foot-five enforcer who’s built like a refrigerator.
In pitch rooms, there’s no deafening air horn and no flashing scoreboard. The loss is quieter because it manifests as polite disengagement. That’s why the feedback often sounds encouraging, even when it’s a clear no:

“This is really interesting work.”
“You clearly know the space well.”
“I’m not sure it fits our focus right now, but it’s thoughtful work.”

A pitch is not a poster, paper, or grant application.
So, what is it?

title: It’s Not That Kind of Meeting
section: What The Room Decides About You
text:
What The Room Decides About You
Ideas evolve. Markets shift. Assumptions fail. Everyone in the room knows that. If academia is the classical physics of the proven, capital is the dark matter of the possible—synthesizing empirical data with the unquantifiable gravity of markets.
That is to say, investors aren’t just assessing your work’s empiricism, they’re also assessing you.
When founders talk about tradeoffs; when they demonstrate their priorities when time runs short; when they can acknowledge uncertainty without losing their footing; whether their answers suggest they’ve navigated difficulties before, or if this is the first real test.
Investors hunt for these signals- any information which gives investors an edge is information they’ll leverage: in what founders emphasize, what they skip, in how they handle questions when the conversation veers off script. It’s all fair game.
This is not because the audience is ignoring the idea. It’s because the idea is solid and, for an investor to conduct their due diligence, they have to test everything else- especially you.

title: How (Some) American Institutions Are Making Startups at Lightspeed
text:
Each year, research universities across the U.S. generate more than 1,000 startup companies armed with cutting-edge technology to outcompete a fast-moving market. Normally, the complexity of negotiations required to finalize the IP licensing wouldn’t be that far removed from a Wall-street M&A deal- so how did UVA and Virginia Tech shorten their IP licensing process from university-standard timelines (~9–12 months) to ~30–60 days?
In my role as Program Manager for Georgetown Tech Ventures within Georgetown’s Office of Technology Commercialization (OTC), I’ve seen multiple successful startups to market, and regardless whether they’re founded by faculty, PhD candidates, or even graduate students, the highest-performing teams approached licensing as an opportunity for strategic alignment rather than a bureaucratic hurdle to rush through.
I’ve seen countless times how the most consistent inflection point in a startup’s journey is the initial licensing of university technology—the moment when IP ownership, growth milestones, and estimated royalty payments converge. It’s only when all those contentious pieces align, a startup can finally raise capital, recruit a team, and set course for market impact.
That’s why we’re excited about Express Licensing; a more resource-efficient, strategically relevant, and robust IP licensing framework for getting cutting-edge research to market, faster.

title: How (Some) American Institutions Are Making Startups at Lightspeed
section: Licensing and Startup Activity in Numbers
text:
Licensing and Startup Activity in Numbers
Express Licensing frameworks are maturing in the midst of robust tech-transfer activity, signaling both demand and opportunity for founders and investors alike:

National data shows U.S. universities issued ~8,800 new licenses for research-generated IP in 2021, and the share going to startups and small firms grew from 69% to 78% over a decade. Between 2015 and 2021, more than 1,000 startups licensed and commercialized academic technology each year.
Meanwhile, the AUTM’s (Association of University Technology Managers) 2024 Licensing Activity Survey shows that technology transfer offices continue to manage “high volumes of disclosures, licensing activity, and startup formation even as funding dynamics and staffing pressures evolve.”

It’s in this high-volume context that Express Licensing offers an additional efficiency-boost: it gives licensing offices the slack to meet new, potential-licensees without sacrificing their time with their existing startup portfolio. While it is a significant investment to stand-up an Express License program, the most successful universities have been reaping dividends year-over-year:

At The University of Georgia (UGA), the Innovation Gateway runs the Georgia Startup License, a structured program that combines market-readiness assessments with Express Licensing agreements. UGA staff and mentors help founders execute a gap analysis and action plan, place founders in programming such as I-Corps, and after satisfying predefined requirements, startups unlock an Express License with increasingly favorable terms, including discounted incubator space and priority support services.
At the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), the Technology Licensing Office (TLO) offers Expedited Licenses, especially for software and other standardized intellectual property categories. Interested parties agree to MIT’s pre-set terms and simply return a signed agreement, which greatly reduces back-and-forth negotiation and maintains robust transparency and clear expectations.

Express Licensing frameworks give startups confidence in their IP licensing early in the process, and that confidence is vital: it allows founders to fundraise, develop new business, and surpass their operational milestones without spending the egregious amount of time it traditionally takes on licensing-contract negotiation.
Express Licensing and Standard Terms work because they give startups, investors, and institutions the confidence to partner before they even meet.

title: How (Some) American Institutions Are Making Startups at Lightspeed
section: Statewide and Multi-Institution Approaches: Virginia’s Lab-to-Launch
text:
Statewide and Multi-Institution Approaches: Virginia’s Lab-to-Launch
Having seen the success on individual campuses, entire states are aligning practices across their research-institutions to strengthen licensing frameworks at scale:
In Virginia, the Lab-to-Launch initiative brings together six R1 research universities—including the University of Virginia (UVA) and Virginia Tech—with the VIPC | Virginia Innovation Partnership Corporation to accelerate research commercialization. The effort hinges on the Virginia Fast-Track License, a standardized IP licensing process that shortens traditional timelines (~9–12 months) to ~30–60 days, eliminates upfront and annual fees for adopting startups, and qualifies eligible startups for up to $50,000 in grant funding..
By standardizing terms across multiple universities; proactively engaging startups; making investment-grade IP digital, searchable, and accessible; and providing entrepreneur-in-residence support; Lab-to-Launch builds clarity, predictability, and ecosystem alignment—the rocket-fuel equivalent for launching startups..
Standardized Terms build predictability, clarity, and ecosystem alignment. The by-product is unparalleled speed.

title: How (Some) American Institutions Are Making Startups at Lightspeed
section: The Evolving Role of Licensing Offices: UVA’s IP Commercialization Model
text:
The Evolving Role of Licensing Offices: UVA’s IP Commercialization Model
Licensing offices today often go far beyond executing agreements, often acting as a cadre of intra-organizational consultants: The Licensing & Ventures Group (LVG) at UVA partners with faculty, researchers, and investors to bring technologies to the marketplace by managing patents, negotiating licenses, and connecting innovators to resources like I-Corps training and seed funding networks.
Rather than treating licensing as an administrative hurdle, UVA’s LVG functions as a strategic partner, guiding inventors and startup teams through commercialization workflows, attracting capital, and helping founders align technical development with market needs. This integration of strategy and execution is designed to support startup success holistically.

title: About

About this site

title: Event Planning Is Not Innovation
text:
From Activity to Judgment
Universities love pipelines because pipelines feel fair — and because they come with a comforting logic: put enough things in, keep them moving, and something good is bound to come out the other end. It sounds rational. It sounds efficient.

It sounds like progress. It isn’t.

Commercialization is not a conveyor belt. It’s not forward motion by default. It’s a series of uncomfortable, explicit decisions made under uncertainty. It requires someone — or some system — to look at the evidence and say: this moves forward. This doesn’t. This goes here. This stops.
Commercialization is a set of gated decisions under uncertainty:

Is this worth advancing?
Which pathway makes sense right now — license, startup, strategic partnership, or stop?
What is the next value inflection point?
Who has to believe what, by when, for this to work?
And who, specifically, is going to pay for it?

A serious commercialization system is designed to answer those questions repeatedly, quickly, and with evidence.
And this is where some university innovation activity tends to miss the mark.
It’s easier to schedule the next workshop than it is to force a meaningful decision. It’s safer to move a project into the next cohort than to decide whether it should exist at all. Activity feels productive, it generates updates, it fills dashboards, and it fools everyone into feeling engaged.
When a university treats programming and activity as an outcomes, everything becomes a KPI. Everything counts as progress. The pitch night counts. The fireside chat counts. The networking cocktail hour counts.
And because all of these count, no one bothers to ask the harder questions:

What risk was reduced?
What belief was validated?
What capital was committed?
What changed?

If the answer is nothing changed except the calendar, then you’ve planned a wedding without vows — beautifully staged, masterfully orchestrated, and fundamentally empty.

title: Event Planning Is Not Innovation
text:
Progress Over Production Value
There are commercialization/innovation systems that tilt toward decisions and outcomes — rather than optics and attendance.
For example, there are dedicated proof-of-concept funding models that lean on staged, evidence-based gates. Programs, such as the NIH Proof-of-Concept Centers1, require projects to tie funding to defined technical and commercial milestones — actual prototypes, feasibility data, and market engagement — before advancement is allowed.

Support isn’t awarded because a workshop was full; it’s awarded because real evidence of commercial potential exists AND risk has been meaningfully reduced. This kind of gate-driven progression tends to produce more actual licensing outcomes, not just piles of post-event clickbait.
That’s what gating looks like. That’s decision infrastructure.

title: Event Planning Is Not Innovation
text:
Decisions, Decisions, Decisions,
Technology Transfer, Technology Development, and Technology Commercialization Offices (TTOs/OTD/OTCs) inside universities are supposed to serve the same role — not as programming machines but as decision makers.
A 2024 study exploring the current state of technology transfer in the U.S.2 finds that offices that emphasize structured engagement and market connection strategies tend to be more effective in bridging research and markets than those that don’t. For example, of those high preforming offices:

~35% use customized licensing agreements tailored to specific industry needs,
~30% have collaborative research ventures to align academic output with commercial partners’ most pressing issue,
and ~20% have industry-sponsored research programs that embed external incentives early,

Some offices have even redesigned the licensing gate itself1. A handful of TTOs, like the University of Cincinnati3 and the University of Missouri System4, have developed express licensing mechanisms — standardized, take-it-or-leave-it agreements with predefined terms — to cut out negotiation drag and get technologies into industry hands faster. These aren’t fancy events with cranberry baked brie bites — they’re structural interventions designed to force a decision about whether a technology will move forward or not, without weeks of back-and-forth over terms.



University of Cincinnati


The Association of University Technology Managers (AUTM) 2024 Licensing Activity Survey5 demonstrates real commercialization outcomes tell the most meaningful story. Research institutions continued to navigate funding shifts and strategic uncertainty while producing measurable market outcomes.
U.S. research expenditures reached roughly $109 billion, underlining the scale of raw innovation output. Invention disclosures continued to climb; and institutions reported steady startup formation and new products entering the market during the reporting period. Universities reported:

26,196 invention disclosures
14,432 new U.S. patent applications filed

— the idea machine is humming. But that’s only half the story. The real signal shows up here:

9,507 licenses and options executed,
775 new products created, and
951 startups formed.

That’s the difference between programming and productivity. One fills seats. The other produces outcomes.
When a TTO builds structured decision points — milestones that reduce technical risk, demonstrate market interest, and negotiate commitment — it makes progress. The AUTM survey5 shows that offices leaning into those practices continue generating startups, licenses, and other real, quantifiable outcomes.

title: Event Planning Is Not Innovation
text:
Footnotes


They’re not included in this article, but we’ve previously explored how both MIT, UVA, and University of Georgia also utilize these strategies for similarly improved results.↩︎

title: H. R. 104
text:
Official Transcript of the Committee on the Socio-Political Fabric, Rayburn House Office Building, Room 2123, Faithfully Recorded and Annotated for Posterity1




The Rayburn House Office Building had been constructed in 1965 by a government that believed, with the particular optimism of the postwar era, that the future would require a great many large rooms for important meetings. It had accordingly been given large rooms. The meetings were still happening. The question of whether they were important was one the building had learned, over sixty years, not to ask.2
The fluorescent lights in Room 2123 hummed at a hypnotic frequency of propagated institutional despair.






CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE (R-IL): A Congressman of the libertarian-adjacent persuasion who had read the Constitution once and felt personally wronged by approximately sixty percent of it. He shuffled papers the way other men did push-ups: compulsively, and with the suggestion of unresolved aggression.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT (D-DE): A five-term Senator that inspired the hit reality television show, “Survivor”. He had outlasted four presidents, three recessions, and one very serious ethics inquiry by simply never doing anything that couldn’t be described as “measured” by pundits his donors watched regularly. His portfolio was up 22% this quarter, mostly in surveillance infrastructure and artisanal bunker manufacturing.3 His tie was the color of compromise. He had worn it to four funerals and twelve fundraising dinners and had stopped feeling the difference around the third funeral.




ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO, of Brooklyn, New York: Unaffiliated voter, nonprofit management studies graduate dressed in the specific way of a person who has thought carefully about appearing to not have thought carefully about it. He had a prepared statement. He had bullet points. He had, in the second paragraph, a genuinely insightful observation about closed primary systems that no one in this room was going to quote. He had also, though he would not have admitted this, a faint and stubborn hope. He carried it the way you carry an umbrella in a city where it almost never rains: awkwardly, somewhat embarrassingly, and unwilling to leave it behind.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK, of Baton Rouge, Louisiana: millennial, twelve-year hospitality veteran, driver of a 2009 Chevy Silverado with a cracked windshield that State Farm had classified as “cosmetic damage” on three consecutive claims.4 She had a manila folder containing three denials, two appeals, one letter from a state regulator that said essentially nothing in four paragraphs, and a printout of Louisiana House Bill 423 annotated in red pen with words that were, technically, opinions. She had placed the folder on the table in front of her- both hands rested upon on it like a executioner resting their hands on the pommel of an axe. She had the expression of a person who had driven seventeen hours and was, therefore, done with preamble.





CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will come to order. “Order”, in terms of the “ordering” principle of this institution appears to be the organized redistribution of wealth from people with the least- to the people who have the most, while we all describe it as something else. I recognize the Senator from Delaware.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. It is a pleasure to be here, or more precisely, it is a necessity to be seen here, a distinction this institution has long since stopped making. The American people—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —are the backbone of this great nation—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: My windshield has a crack in it the size of my forearm and State Farm called it cosmetic damage.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —and your voices are essential to the democratic—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I have the letters.
[Witness places a manila folder on the table. The Senator leaned slightly toward his aide.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (to aide) Can we get her a sedative? Or a cabinet position in the Department of Agriculture?
[The aide typed something. Nothing happened. Such is the nature of the Department of Agriculture.]
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Let’s swear in our witnesses and proceed. We have limited time before the next recess, a subsequent recess, and a vote on something that will be described as historic and will not be.




CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Mr. El Gourdo, you may proceed.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Thank you. I’ll be brief because I have to get back to Brooklyn to confirm whether my landlord raised my rent during this opening statement, which is not a joke, or not entirely a joke, which is itself a kind of joke.
I, like some 1.1 million unaffiliated voters in New York City — the fastest-growing registration category in the state, are locked out of a closed primary system that decides every meaningful race before the rest of us see a ballot. We are highly educated, embedded in the city, and entirely alienated from the binary machinery of modern American politics. Sixty-five percent of voters believe Congress is out of touch with ordinary people. I want to say, for the record, that I think that number is optimistic.
Specifically today, I want to talk about what it actually costs to build something in this country. I tried to start a nonprofit — urban gardening, community food access, nothing that should frighten anyone. The average time to obtain the relevant permits in New York City is fourteen months.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Wonderful. And you’re from — Queens?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Brooklyn.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (nodding in a way that suggested Brooklyn confirmed something he had long suspected about Brooklyn) Mm. I had a very good piece of salmon there in 2019. Great borough.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Fourteen months, Senator. The average small business in America fails within its first year. We are regulating people out of their own ambitions before they have sold a single thing. The system does not feel like a right. It feels like a subscription service I cannot afford, administered by people who have never tried to use it.5
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: You mentioned fourteen months. You know how long it takes to register a business in Estonia?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: …No?
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Eighteen minutes. Online. (produces a printed Wikipedia page with the practiced efficiency of a man who keeps Wikipedia printouts the way other men keep business cards) Estonia looked at the administrative state and concluded: what if we simply didn’t. And now they have a functional digital society and we have fourteen months and a drainage assessment fee.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I don’t know why Estonia is always the example.
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Because Estonia got it right and it haunts me.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Fair.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: I think we can all agree that the regulatory environment presents certain structural challenges that a bipartisan framework could—
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: My father’s venture capital firm did an internal analysis last year. The best return on investment in their entire portfolio was regulatory influence. Buying access to permitting agencies. Better than any direct investment in an actual small business. He showed me the numbers. He thought it was funny.
[A brief silence, during which the Senator found several things to look at that were not Mr. El Gourdo.]




CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Ms. Monster Truck. The floor is yours.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Are we done with the polite part? Good.
[She opened the folder.]
I’ve been in hospitality for twelve years. I know food. I know how to run a line at full capacity on a Saturday night when the dishwasher quits and the health inspector walks in at seven pm. My sister and I have been planning a food truck for three years. Po-boys and smoked boudin. Something real. Something ours.
We saved seventeen thousand dollars. We found a truck. We started pulling permits.
Louisiana requires a state health certificate, a city business license, a fire inspection certification, and proof of a licensed commissary kitchen — which can’t be substituted with your own kitchen regardless of its equipment, because the certification costs four thousand dollars and takes four to seven months. Then a Department of Revenue seller’s permit, a sales tax registration, and in New Orleans, a separate French Quarter vendor permit for any street within two blocks that costs twelve hundred dollars and has a waiting list that has not moved since 2022.
[She turned a page.]
Meanwhile, Governor Landry signs the “No Pay, No Play” insurance reform. The $100,000 auto threshold. The Housley Presumption, gone. You tell us it’s to prevent frivolous lawsuits. My premiums went up fifteen percent the following quarter. I am currently deciding between fixing my alternator and buying insulin, which is not a choice that should exist in a country with this much money in it, and yet here we are, and there it is.
You want to talk about why turnout in Louisiana was twenty-three percent? That is why. Because the people who were supposed to represent us are busy being the HR department for a company that’s going bankrupt, and some of us have started to notice.
[She closed the folder.]
That is what I wanted to say about the right to start a business.




[A silence settled over the room of the kind that forms when truth has been said in a place that is not accustomed to it. The fluorescent lights hummed. They had hummed through hearings on Vietnam, Watergate, the deficit, healthcare, the financial collapse, and a great many working groups. They intended to keep humming. They had no opinions. This was, in its way, a comfort, and also not a comfort at all.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: The commissary kitchen requirement is — yes, that’s something we’ve been examining—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: With what outcome?
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —as part of a broader working group—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: What working group?
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —that is currently in the process of convening—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (quietly) Yes. You’ve mentioned.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I’ll keep mentioning it.
[The Senator looked at the folder. He had developed, over his long time in politics, a very precise internal instrument for detecting when a moment required the appearance of being moved by it. He had also a second, smaller, considerably less-exercised instrument that occasionally registered when he was actually moved by something. The second instrument had not had much practice. The commissary kitchen requirement was doing something to the second instrument. He was not entirely sure how he felt about this.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (to aide, very quietly) Find out which subcommittee handles commissary kitchen certification requirements.
[The aide looked this up. It was four subcommittees. The Senator chose not to mention this.]
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Here is what I keep arriving at. You both came here. You know how this system works — or doesn’t — better than most of the people in this building who are paid to know it. You’re asking the machinery of the state to get out of the way of the thing you’re willing to bleed for. That should be — it should be — the most obvious, least partisan, most achievable thing we could agree on.
[He looked at the Senator.]
And instead it’s going to go to a working group, and then a subcommittee, and then a recess, and then an election cycle, and somewhere in Louisiana a man who owns a yacht called Tax Shelter is going to file his quarterly returns in a condition of profound personal comfort, and he will not think about the food truck once.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: That is an uncharitable characterization of the legislative process.
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Is it wrong?
[The lights hummed.]
[Somewhere down the hall, a lobbyist was having an excellent lunch and not thinking about kitchens.]
[The Senator did not answer.]
[This was, in its way, an answer.]
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: (quietly, to Princess Monster Truck) We don’t want a reboot, you know. We just want a door that isn’t locked from the inside.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: (equally quietly) And fix my damn insurance before I lose my mind.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Also that.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Is the Estonia thing real?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: It’s Wikipedia. So. Possibly.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I’ll take possibly.




CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will take these testimonies under advisement. We will produce a white paper. It will be twelve pages. Seven people will read past page four. The rest of the country will continue to stare at the flickering lights of their dwindling bank accounts and draw their own conclusions, several of which will be correct. Meeting adjourned.7






On the train back to Brooklyn — delayed again, indefinitely, for reasons the MTA described as “signal issues” and everyone understood to mean something much older and less fixable — Zucchini El Gourdo wrote a letter. Not a policy brief. Not a Substack post. A letter, to someone who knew a city councilperson who had been, apparently, asking why the unaffiliated numbers kept climbing and nobody showed up. He was not, he thought, hopeful exactly. He was something in the neighborhood of hopeful. Close enough to feel the weather.




On the drive back to Louisiana, Princess Monster Truck called her sister.
“We’re still doing it,” she said.
“After all that?”
“Especially after all that. But I need you to look into what they actually enforce on commissary certification versus what’s technically required. Because I have a feeling those are two very different numbers.”
Her sister laughed. It was the laugh of someone who had known this voice for thirty years and recognized what it sounded like when it got like this: past angry, into something harder and more durable than angry, something that had driven seventeen hours and brought the folder and was going to keep mentioning it until something moved.
The crack in the windshield caught the last of the sunset at an angle that turned it briefly into something that looked almost like a map.
Maps are just descriptions of where you are.
What matters is where you’re going.

The food truck opened in the spring. It was called THE SEVENTEENTH HOUR. The boudin was excellent. A man from a food safety publication ate there on the third week and wrote a piece about how commissary kitchen certification requirements in Louisiana were being enforced inconsistently across parishes in ways that suggested the regulation itself was due for review. Two thousand people read it. One was on a working group.

This is not how change happens.
Except that sometimes, rarely, against considerable odds and the general institutional preference for things remaining as they are, it is.
Somewhere in Delaware, Senator Shmurple-Berry Yogurt’s second instrument — the small one, the underexercised one — registered something it couldn’t quite name. He didn’t do anything about it. Not immediately. But the following Tuesday he ate a po-boy from a food truck parked outside the Capitol in contravention of approximately four separate permitting regulations. It was very good. He did not mention the permits.
This was not progress.
It was also not nothing.
The lights hummed on.
They always do.

title: H. R. 104
text:
The Rayburn House Office Building had been constructed in 1965 by a government that believed, with the particular optimism of the postwar era, that the future would require a great many large rooms for important meetings. It had accordingly been given large rooms. The meetings were still happening. The question of whether they were important was one the building had learned, over sixty years, not to ask.2
The fluorescent lights in Room 2123 hummed at a hypnotic frequency of propagated institutional despair.

title: H. R. 104
text:
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE (R-IL): A Congressman of the libertarian-adjacent persuasion who had read the Constitution once and felt personally wronged by approximately sixty percent of it. He shuffled papers the way other men did push-ups: compulsively, and with the suggestion of unresolved aggression.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT (D-DE): A five-term Senator that inspired the hit reality television show, “Survivor”. He had outlasted four presidents, three recessions, and one very serious ethics inquiry by simply never doing anything that couldn’t be described as “measured” by pundits his donors watched regularly. His portfolio was up 22% this quarter, mostly in surveillance infrastructure and artisanal bunker manufacturing.3 His tie was the color of compromise. He had worn it to four funerals and twelve fundraising dinners and had stopped feeling the difference around the third funeral.




ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO, of Brooklyn, New York: Unaffiliated voter, nonprofit management studies graduate dressed in the specific way of a person who has thought carefully about appearing to not have thought carefully about it. He had a prepared statement. He had bullet points. He had, in the second paragraph, a genuinely insightful observation about closed primary systems that no one in this room was going to quote. He had also, though he would not have admitted this, a faint and stubborn hope. He carried it the way you carry an umbrella in a city where it almost never rains: awkwardly, somewhat embarrassingly, and unwilling to leave it behind.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK, of Baton Rouge, Louisiana: millennial, twelve-year hospitality veteran, driver of a 2009 Chevy Silverado with a cracked windshield that State Farm had classified as “cosmetic damage” on three consecutive claims.4 She had a manila folder containing three denials, two appeals, one letter from a state regulator that said essentially nothing in four paragraphs, and a printout of Louisiana House Bill 423 annotated in red pen with words that were, technically, opinions. She had placed the folder on the table in front of her- both hands rested upon on it like a executioner resting their hands on the pommel of an axe. She had the expression of a person who had driven seventeen hours and was, therefore, done with preamble.

title: H. R. 104
text:
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will come to order. “Order”, in terms of the “ordering” principle of this institution appears to be the organized redistribution of wealth from people with the least- to the people who have the most, while we all describe it as something else. I recognize the Senator from Delaware.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. It is a pleasure to be here, or more precisely, it is a necessity to be seen here, a distinction this institution has long since stopped making. The American people—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —are the backbone of this great nation—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: My windshield has a crack in it the size of my forearm and State Farm called it cosmetic damage.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —and your voices are essential to the democratic—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I have the letters.
[Witness places a manila folder on the table. The Senator leaned slightly toward his aide.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (to aide) Can we get her a sedative? Or a cabinet position in the Department of Agriculture?
[The aide typed something. Nothing happened. Such is the nature of the Department of Agriculture.]
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Let’s swear in our witnesses and proceed. We have limited time before the next recess, a subsequent recess, and a vote on something that will be described as historic and will not be.

title: H. R. 104
text:
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Mr. El Gourdo, you may proceed.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Thank you. I’ll be brief because I have to get back to Brooklyn to confirm whether my landlord raised my rent during this opening statement, which is not a joke, or not entirely a joke, which is itself a kind of joke.
I, like some 1.1 million unaffiliated voters in New York City — the fastest-growing registration category in the state, are locked out of a closed primary system that decides every meaningful race before the rest of us see a ballot. We are highly educated, embedded in the city, and entirely alienated from the binary machinery of modern American politics. Sixty-five percent of voters believe Congress is out of touch with ordinary people. I want to say, for the record, that I think that number is optimistic.
Specifically today, I want to talk about what it actually costs to build something in this country. I tried to start a nonprofit — urban gardening, community food access, nothing that should frighten anyone. The average time to obtain the relevant permits in New York City is fourteen months.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Wonderful. And you’re from — Queens?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Brooklyn.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (nodding in a way that suggested Brooklyn confirmed something he had long suspected about Brooklyn) Mm. I had a very good piece of salmon there in 2019. Great borough.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Fourteen months, Senator. The average small business in America fails within its first year. We are regulating people out of their own ambitions before they have sold a single thing. The system does not feel like a right. It feels like a subscription service I cannot afford, administered by people who have never tried to use it.5
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: You mentioned fourteen months. You know how long it takes to register a business in Estonia?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: …No?
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Eighteen minutes. Online. (produces a printed Wikipedia page with the practiced efficiency of a man who keeps Wikipedia printouts the way other men keep business cards) Estonia looked at the administrative state and concluded: what if we simply didn’t. And now they have a functional digital society and we have fourteen months and a drainage assessment fee.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I don’t know why Estonia is always the example.
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Because Estonia got it right and it haunts me.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Fair.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: I think we can all agree that the regulatory environment presents certain structural challenges that a bipartisan framework could—
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: My father’s venture capital firm did an internal analysis last year. The best return on investment in their entire portfolio was regulatory influence. Buying access to permitting agencies. Better than any direct investment in an actual small business. He showed me the numbers. He thought it was funny.
[A brief silence, during which the Senator found several things to look at that were not Mr. El Gourdo.]

title: H. R. 104
text:
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Ms. Monster Truck. The floor is yours.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Are we done with the polite part? Good.
[She opened the folder.]
I’ve been in hospitality for twelve years. I know food. I know how to run a line at full capacity on a Saturday night when the dishwasher quits and the health inspector walks in at seven pm. My sister and I have been planning a food truck for three years. Po-boys and smoked boudin. Something real. Something ours.
We saved seventeen thousand dollars. We found a truck. We started pulling permits.
Louisiana requires a state health certificate, a city business license, a fire inspection certification, and proof of a licensed commissary kitchen — which can’t be substituted with your own kitchen regardless of its equipment, because the certification costs four thousand dollars and takes four to seven months. Then a Department of Revenue seller’s permit, a sales tax registration, and in New Orleans, a separate French Quarter vendor permit for any street within two blocks that costs twelve hundred dollars and has a waiting list that has not moved since 2022.
[She turned a page.]
Meanwhile, Governor Landry signs the “No Pay, No Play” insurance reform. The $100,000 auto threshold. The Housley Presumption, gone. You tell us it’s to prevent frivolous lawsuits. My premiums went up fifteen percent the following quarter. I am currently deciding between fixing my alternator and buying insulin, which is not a choice that should exist in a country with this much money in it, and yet here we are, and there it is.
You want to talk about why turnout in Louisiana was twenty-three percent? That is why. Because the people who were supposed to represent us are busy being the HR department for a company that’s going bankrupt, and some of us have started to notice.
[She closed the folder.]
That is what I wanted to say about the right to start a business.

title: H. R. 104
text:
[A silence settled over the room of the kind that forms when truth has been said in a place that is not accustomed to it. The fluorescent lights hummed. They had hummed through hearings on Vietnam, Watergate, the deficit, healthcare, the financial collapse, and a great many working groups. They intended to keep humming. They had no opinions. This was, in its way, a comfort, and also not a comfort at all.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: The commissary kitchen requirement is — yes, that’s something we’ve been examining—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: With what outcome?
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —as part of a broader working group—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: What working group?
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —that is currently in the process of convening—
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (quietly) Yes. You’ve mentioned.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I’ll keep mentioning it.
[The Senator looked at the folder. He had developed, over his long time in politics, a very precise internal instrument for detecting when a moment required the appearance of being moved by it. He had also a second, smaller, considerably less-exercised instrument that occasionally registered when he was actually moved by something. The second instrument had not had much practice. The commissary kitchen requirement was doing something to the second instrument. He was not entirely sure how he felt about this.]
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (to aide, very quietly) Find out which subcommittee handles commissary kitchen certification requirements.
[The aide looked this up. It was four subcommittees. The Senator chose not to mention this.]
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Here is what I keep arriving at. You both came here. You know how this system works — or doesn’t — better than most of the people in this building who are paid to know it. You’re asking the machinery of the state to get out of the way of the thing you’re willing to bleed for. That should be — it should be — the most obvious, least partisan, most achievable thing we could agree on.
[He looked at the Senator.]
And instead it’s going to go to a working group, and then a subcommittee, and then a recess, and then an election cycle, and somewhere in Louisiana a man who owns a yacht called Tax Shelter is going to file his quarterly returns in a condition of profound personal comfort, and he will not think about the food truck once.
SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: That is an uncharitable characterization of the legislative process.
CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Is it wrong?
[The lights hummed.]
[Somewhere down the hall, a lobbyist was having an excellent lunch and not thinking about kitchens.]
[The Senator did not answer.]
[This was, in its way, an answer.]
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: (quietly, to Princess Monster Truck) We don’t want a reboot, you know. We just want a door that isn’t locked from the inside.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: (equally quietly) And fix my damn insurance before I lose my mind.
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Also that.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Is the Estonia thing real?
MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: It’s Wikipedia. So. Possibly.
PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I’ll take possibly.

title: H. R. 104
text: CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will take these testimonies under advisement. We will produce a white paper. It will be twelve pages. Seven people will read past page four. The rest of the country will continue to stare at the flickering lights of their dwindling bank accounts and draw their own conclusions, several of which will be correct. Meeting adjourned.7

title: H. R. 104
text:
On the train back to Brooklyn — delayed again, indefinitely, for reasons the MTA described as “signal issues” and everyone understood to mean something much older and less fixable — Zucchini El Gourdo wrote a letter. On the drive back to Louisiana, Princess Monster Truck called her sister.

“After all that?”
“Especially after all that. But I need you to look into what they actually enforce on commissary certification versus what’s technically required. Because I have a feeling those are two very different numbers.”
Her sister laughed. It was the laugh of someone who had known this voice for thirty years and recognized what it sounded like when it got like this: past angry, into something harder and more durable than angry, something that had driven seventeen hours and brought the folder and was going to keep mentioning it until something moved.
The crack in the windshield caught the last of the sunset at an angle that turned it briefly into something that looked almost like a map.
Maps are just descriptions of where you are.
What matters is where you’re going.

The food truck opened in the spring. It was called THE SEVENTEENTH HOUR. The boudin was excellent. A man from a food safety publication ate there on the third week and wrote a piece about how commissary kitchen certification requirements in Louisiana were being enforced inconsistently across parishes in ways that suggested the regulation itself was due for review. Two thousand people read it. One was on a working group.

This is not how change happens.
Except that sometimes, rarely, against considerable odds and the general institutional preference for things remaining as they are, it is.
Somewhere in Delaware, Senator Shmurple-Berry Yogurt’s second instrument — the small one, the underexercised one — registered something it couldn’t quite name. He didn’t do anything about it. Not immediately. But the following Tuesday he ate a po-boy from a food truck parked outside the Capitol in contravention of approximately four separate permitting regulations. It was very good. The lights hummed on.
They always do.

title: H. R. 104
text:
Footnotes


Posterity has not yet confirmed whether it wanted this.↩︎
Buildings, if they survive long enough, develop a philosophical detachment about their inhabitants. The Rayburn Building had seen enough to know that the people who came through its doors were, on balance, trying their best, and that this was simultaneously more and less reassuring than it sounded.↩︎
He had found, over a long career, that the key to not thinking about certain things was to simply not think about them. He had refined this into something approaching an art form, or possibly a medical condition. He had not looked into which.↩︎
A cracked windshield is, in point of fact, a structural integrity issue and a safety classification under federal highway standards. State Farm was aware of this. They were counting on Princess Monster Truck not having a lawyer. She had a cousin who had watched a lot of Better Call Saul. This was, everyone agreed, not nothing.↩︎
This was, in fact, an accurate description of several subscription services as well, but that is a matter for a different committee.↩︎
This section is titled “The Synthesis” because congressional transcripts require section headers and “The Part Where Everyone Understands The Problem And Then Does Not Fix It” was considered too long.↩︎
The Committee on the Socio-Political Fabric would reconvene at a date to be determined, pending scheduling, recess, and the outcome of an election that both Zucchini El Gourdo and Princess Monster Truck had, until very recently, every intention of sitting out. The white paper would be filed. The working group would convene, eventually. These things would happen in the order that things happen when no one is in a hurry, which is to say: slowly, and then not at all, and then, occasionally, all at once, in ways that surprised everyone and shouldn’t have.↩︎
